questions.question:
Two finance companies, P and Q, declared fixed annual rates of interest on the amounts invested with them. The rates of interest offered by these companies may differ from year to year. Year-wise annual rates of interest offered by these companies are shown by the line graph below. If the amounts invested in the companies P and Q in 2006 are in the ratio 8 : 9, then the amounts received after one year as interests from P and Q would be in the ratio:

A. 2 : 3
B. 3 : 4
C. 6 : 7
D. 4 : 3
questions.to_find: ratio of interest amounts received from companies P and Q after one year, given their 2006 investment ratio is 8:9

understand.plain: Two companies P and Q offer different annual interest rates each year. In 2006, P offered 6% and Q offered 4% annual interest (read from the line graph). Money is invested in both companies in the ratio 8:9, and we need the ratio of the interest earned after exactly one year. Since interest = Principal x Rate, the ratio of interests is (8 x 6%) : (9 x 4%) = 48 : 36 = 4 : 3.

keywords.explain: A fixed percentage applied to the principal amount for one year; interest earned = Principal x Rate/100
keywords.example: An investment of 8000 at 6% p.a. earns 480 as interest in one year

keywords.explain: The interest rate a company offers changes year to year; the graph shows each year's rate as a plotted point connected by line segments
keywords.example: At year 2006 read the y-value for P and Q from the graph to get that year's rates

keywords.explain: When two investments earn interest at different rates, the ratio of the interest amounts is (Principal_P x Rate_P) : (Principal_Q x Rate_Q)
keywords.example: If 800 at 6% and 900 at 4%, interests are 48 and 36, ratio = 48:36 = 4:3

fill='#D85A30'/><text x='366' y='76' class='sub ink'>Company Q</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Line graph showing P at 6% and Q at 4% annual interest rate in year 2006

given.aim: Find the ratio of interest amounts earned from P and Q after one year for investments made in 2006

terms.meaning: For every 8 rupees invested in P, 9 rupees are invested in Q; let P = 8k and Q = 9k for some constant k
terms.example: If k = 1000, then P = 8000 and Q = 9000 are invested
terms.connects: These are the principal amounts on which interest is calculated

terms.term: Interest rate of P in 2006 = 6% per annum
terms.meaning: Company P offers 6% annual interest on the invested amount in the year 2006, as read from the line graph
terms.example: An investment of 8000 in P earns 8000 x 6/100 = 480 as interest
terms.connects: Multiplied with P's principal to get P's interest contribution

terms.term: Interest rate of Q in 2006 = 4% per annum
terms.meaning: Company Q offers 4% annual interest on the invested amount in the year 2006, as read from the line graph
terms.example: An investment of 9000 in Q earns 9000 x 4/100 = 360 as interest
terms.connects: Multiplied with Q's principal to get Q's interest contribution

given.plan: Use Simple Interest = Principal x Rate/100. With P = 8k and Q = 9k, compute Interest_P = 8k x 6/100 and Interest_Q = 9k x 4/100, then simplify the ratio.

steps.title: Express principals using the given ratio
steps.formula_raw: Let P = 8k and Q = 9k for some positive constant k
steps.apply: Since investments are in ratio 8:9, set principal in P = 8k and principal in Q = 9k
steps.note: The constant k cancels when forming the ratio of interests

steps.title: Read interest rates from graph for year 2006
steps.formula_raw: Rate_P = 6% p.a., Rate_Q = 4% p.a. (from line graph at year 2006)
steps.apply: At x = 2006, the graph shows P's line at y = 6 and Q's line at y = 4
steps.note: Always read graph values carefully; the two lines represent the two companies

steps.title: Calculate interest for each company
steps.note: Both interests are proportional to k, so k will cancel in the ratio

steps.title: Form and simplify the ratio of interests
steps.apply: Cancel common factor k/100: ratio = 48 : 36. Divide both by 12: 48/12 = 4, 36/12 = 3. Final ratio = 4 : 3
steps.note: Always verify by checking GCD: gcd(48, 36) = 12, giving the simplified form 4:3

solution.result: The ratio of interest amounts from P and Q is 4 : 3, which matches option D.

questions.formula_note: Simple interest formula (Interest = Principal x Rate/100) is not in the registry and is added to the formulas array.

questions.question:
The growth of bacteria (lactobacillus) in milk leads to curd formation. A minimum bacterial population density of 0.8 (in suitable units) is needed to form curd. In the graph below, the population density of lactobacillus in 1 litre of milk is plotted as a function of time, at two different temperatures, 25 degrees C and 37 degrees C.

Consider the following statements based on the data shown above:

i. The growth in bacterial population stops earlier at 37 degrees C as compared to 25 degrees C.
ii. The time taken for curd formation at 25 degrees C is twice the time taken at 37 degrees C.

Which one of the following options is correct?

A. Only i
B. Only ii
C. Both i and ii
D. Neither i nor ii
questions.to_find: which of the two statements about bacterial growth at 25 degrees C and 37 degrees C is/are correct

understand.plain: The graph plots bacterial population density vs. time for two temperature curves (25°C and 37°C). At higher temperature (37°C), bacteria grow faster but also stop growing (plateau) earlier in time. Statement i says the plateau occurs earlier at 37°C - this is true from the graph. Statement ii says the curd-formation time at 25°C is exactly twice that at 37°C - reading the graph shows the ratio is NOT exactly 2, so statement ii is false. Only statement i is correct.

keywords.explain: The minimum bacterial count (0.8 units per litre) needed for curd to form; time to reach this level differs by temperature
keywords.example: If at 37°C the density reaches 0.8 at t = 3 h and at 25°C at t = 5 h, the ratio is 5/3, not 2

keywords.explain: The point where the bacterial population stops increasing and levels off; higher temperature causes an earlier plateau
keywords.example: At 37°C the curve flattens at t = 8 h; at 25°C it continues rising until t = 12 h — so the plateau is earlier at 37°C

keywords.explain: Higher temperature accelerates enzymatic reactions in bacteria, increasing growth rate but also shortening the active growth phase
keywords.example: The 37°C curve rises more steeply and peaks sooner than the 25°C curve

380,57 420,57 460,57 495,57' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5'/><polyline points='55,195 150,188 220,172 290,148 350,125 400,95 440,72 470,62 495,60' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='83' y='54' class='sub ink'>37 degrees C</text><rect x='65' y='63' width='14' height='4' rx='2' fill='#1D9E75'/><text x='83' y='69' class='sub ink'>25 degrees C</text><line x1='240' y1='125' x2='240' y2='195' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='238' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='#D85A30'>t1</text><line x1='350' y1='125' x2='350' y2='195' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='350' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='#1D9E75'>t2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph showing bacterial population density vs time for 37°C curve (steeper, plateaus earlier) and 25°C curve (slower, plateaus later), with threshold line at density 0.8

given.aim: Determine which statements correctly describe the bacterial growth data at 25°C and 37°C from the graph

terms.term: Minimum density for curd formation = 0.8 units/litre
terms.meaning: The bacterial population must reach at least 0.8 units per litre before the milk can set into curd; the time to first reach this value on the graph is the curd-formation time
terms.example: Read off the time at which each temperature curve crosses the horizontal line y = 0.8
terms.connects: Used to evaluate statement ii about relative curd-formation times

terms.meaning: The maximum population density at which bacterial growth stops; shown as the flat (horizontal) part of each curve on the graph
terms.example: The 37°C curve flattens out at an earlier time than the 25°C curve
terms.connects: Used to evaluate statement i about which temperature stops growing earlier

terms.meaning: Each curve on the graph represents population density as a function of time at one fixed temperature; higher temperature = faster initial growth but earlier plateau
terms.example: At t = 5 hours the 37°C curve may already be plateauing while the 25°C curve is still rising
terms.connects: Both statements are evaluated by comparing features of these two curves

given.plan: For statement i: compare the time at which each curve reaches its plateau (flat region). For statement ii: read the time at which each curve first crosses y = 0.8 and check if one is exactly double the other. Use graph readings to confirm or refute each statement.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement i: plateau time comparison
steps.formula_raw: Compare time at which dP/dt = 0 (slope = 0) for 37°C and 25°C curves
steps.apply: From the graph, the 37°C curve visibly flattens and becomes horizontal at a smaller time value than the 25°C curve. The 37°C bacteria stop growing earlier. Statement i is TRUE.
steps.note: Higher temperature accelerates metabolism so bacteria exhaust nutrients faster, causing an earlier plateau

steps.title: Evaluate Statement ii: curd-formation time ratio
steps.formula_raw: t_curd = time when population density first reaches 0.8 units/litre
steps.apply: From the graph, the 37°C curve crosses density = 0.8 at approximately t1 hours, and the 25°C curve crosses at approximately t2 hours. The graph shows t2 is NOT exactly double t1 (the ratio is closer to 1.5 to 1 than 2 to 1). Statement ii is FALSE.
steps.note: The question asks for an exact doubling; from the graph the times are clearly not in a 2:1 ratio

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.formula_raw: Statement i: TRUE. Statement ii: FALSE.
steps.apply: Only statement i is correct, which corresponds to option A.
steps.note: When evaluating data-interpretation questions, carefully read exact values from graph axes rather than estimating visually

solution.result: Only statement i is correct. The growth in bacterial population stops earlier at 37°C than at 25°C. The correct answer is A.

questions.formula_note: No standard mathematical formulas are needed; this question relies on reading and interpreting a line graph correctly.

formulas.name: Simple Interest
formulas.plain: Interest = Principal x Rate / 100; for one year, SI = P * R / 100
